question_html: A rectangular loop of area {tex}\vec{A}{/tex}, carrying current {tex}I{/tex}, is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex}. With the help of a suitable diagram, derive an expression, in vector form, for the torque acting on the loop.
question_text: A rectangular loop of area {tex}\vec{A}{/tex} , carrying current {tex}I{/tex} , is placed in a uniform magnetic field {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} . With the help of a suitable diagram, derive an expression, in vector form, for the torque acting on the loop.
explanation: Forces on the arms BC and DA are, equal opposite and collinear. Hence they will cancel each other. The forces on arms AB and CD are {tex}\vec{F}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} , equal but not collinear. The magnitude of the torque on the loop is {tex} \tau =F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =I a b B \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =m B \sin \theta (m=I A) {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
solution: Forces on the arms BC and DA are, equal opposite and collinear. Hence they will cancel each other. The forces on arms AB and CD are {tex}\vec{F}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex} , equal but not collinear. The magnitude of the torque on the loop is {tex} \tau =F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =I a b B \sin \theta {/tex} {tex} =m B \sin \theta (m=I A) {/tex} {tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}
solution_html:
Forces on the arms BC and DA are, equal opposite and collinear. Hence they will cancel each other.
The forces on arms AB and CD are {tex}\vec{F}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{F}_2{/tex}, equal but not collinear. The magnitude of the torque on the loop is
{tex} \tau =F_1 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} =I a b B \sin \theta {/tex}
{tex} =m B \sin \theta (m=I A) {/tex}
{tex} \vec{\tau} =\vec{m} \times \vec{B} {/tex}

question_text: A short bar magnet of magnetic moment m = 0.32 JT -1 is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.15 T. If the bar is free to rotate in the plane of the field, which orientation would correspond to its stable, and unstable equilibrium? What is the potential energy of the magnet in each case?
explanation: Magnetic Moment of the bar magnet is given by , M = {tex}0.32 J T^{-1}{/tex} External magnetic field is given by, B = 0.15 T For the system to be in stable equilibrium the angle θ , between the bar magnet and the magnetic field is taken as 0°. Potential energy of the system = {tex}-MB\cos\theta{/tex} {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=-4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex} For unstable equilibrium, the bar magnet is oriented at 180°to the magnetic field. θ = 180° Potential energy = – MB cos θ {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}
solution: Magnetic Moment of the bar magnet is given by , M = {tex}0.32 J T^{-1}{/tex} External magnetic field is given by, B = 0.15 T For the system to be in stable equilibrium the angle θ , between the bar magnet and the magnetic field is taken as 0°. Potential energy of the system = {tex}-MB\cos\theta{/tex} {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=-4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex} For unstable equilibrium, the bar magnet is oriented at 180°to the magnetic field. θ = 180° Potential energy = – MB cos θ {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}
solution_html:
Magnetic Moment of the bar magnet is given by , M = {tex}0.32 J T^{-1}{/tex} 
External magnetic field is given by, B = 0.15 T

  1. For the system to be in stable equilibrium the angle θ, between the bar magnet and the magnetic field is taken as 0°.
    Potential energy of the system = {tex}-MB\cos\theta{/tex}
    {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 0^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}=-4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}

  2. For unstable equilibrium, the bar magnet is oriented at 180°to the magnetic field.
    θ = 180°
    Potential energy = – MB cos θ
    {tex}=-0.32 \times 0.15 \cos 180^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}=4.8 \times 10^{-2} J{/tex}

question_html: A bar magnet of dipole moment 3 am2 rests with its centre on a frictionless pivot. A force F is applied at right angles to the axis of the magnet, 10 cm from the pivot. It is observed that an external magnetic field of 0.25 T is required to hold the magnet in equilibrium at an angle of 30o with the field. Calculate the value of F. How will the equilibrium be effected if F is withdrawn?
question_text: A bar magnet of dipole moment 3 am 2 rests with its centre on a frictionless pivot. A force F is applied at right angles to the axis of the magnet, 10 cm from the pivot. It is observed that an external magnetic field of 0.25 T is required to hold the magnet in equilibrium at an angle of 30 o with the field. Calculate the value of F. How will the equilibrium be effected if F is withdrawn?
explanation: Dipole moment of the magnet = M = 3 Am 2 F = force applied at a distance 10 em from the centre It is now in equilibrium at an angle = {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o External magnetic field strength = B = 0.25 T The magnet will be at rest when the total torque acting on it is 0. It means that the torque due to applied force F is equal to the torque due to magnetic force. Torque due to applied force = F {tex}\times{/tex} 10 Torque due to magnetic force = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o Since, torque due to applied force F = Torque due to magnetic force, so F {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o {tex}F=\frac{1}{10} \times 3 \times 0.25 \times \sin 30^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \times 3 \times 0.25 \times 0.5{/tex} = 0.375 N If F is withdrawn, the magnet will go back to its original position.
solution: Dipole moment of the magnet = M = 3 Am 2 F = force applied at a distance 10 em from the centre It is now in equilibrium at an angle = {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o External magnetic field strength = B = 0.25 T The magnet will be at rest when the total torque acting on it is 0. It means that the torque due to applied force F is equal to the torque due to magnetic force. Torque due to applied force = F {tex}\times{/tex} 10 Torque due to magnetic force = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o Since, torque due to applied force F = Torque due to magnetic force, so F {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o {tex}F=\frac{1}{10} \times 3 \times 0.25 \times \sin 30^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \times 3 \times 0.25 \times 0.5{/tex} = 0.375 N If F is withdrawn, the magnet will go back to its original position.
solution_html:
Dipole moment of the magnet = M = 3 Am2
F = force applied at a distance 10 em from the centre
It is now in equilibrium at an angle = {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o
External magnetic field strength = B = 0.25 T 
The magnet will be at rest when the total torque acting on it is 0.
It means that the torque due to applied force F is equal to the torque due to magnetic force.
Torque due to applied force = F {tex}\times{/tex} 10
Torque due to magnetic force = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o
Since, torque due to applied force F = Torque due to magnetic force, so
F {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.25 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o
{tex}F=\frac{1}{10} \times 3 \times 0.25 \times \sin 30^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}=\frac{1}{10} \times 3 \times 0.25 \times 0.5{/tex}
= 0.375 N
If F is withdrawn, the magnet will go back to its original position.

question_html:
A short bar magnet of magnetic moment 0.9 JT-1 is placed with its axis at 30o to a uniform magnetic field. It experiences a torque of 0.063 J. 

  1. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field.
  2. In which orientation will the bar magnet be in stable equilibrium in the magnetic field?
question_text: A short bar magnet of magnetic moment 0.9 JT -1 is placed with its axis at 30 o to a uniform magnetic field. It experiences a torque of 0.063 J. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field. In which orientation will the bar magnet be in stable equilibrium in the magnetic field?
explanation: Magnetic moment M = 0.9 J/T {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.063 J, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o We know {tex}\tau=M \times B{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 0.063 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o B = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.063}{0.9}{/tex} = 0.14 T Stable equilibrium is position of minimum energy. Since {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} U = - MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Where U is the energy stored or P.E. of the magnet inside magnetic field B. So, when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, U = -MB is the minimum energy. Thus, when {tex}\vec M{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are parallel to each other bar magnet is in stable equilibrium.
solution: Magnetic moment M = 0.9 J/T {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.063 J, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o We know {tex}\tau=M \times B{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 0.063 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30 o B = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.063}{0.9}{/tex} = 0.14 T Stable equilibrium is position of minimum energy. Since {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} U = - MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} Where U is the energy stored or P.E. of the magnet inside magnetic field B. So, when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, U = -MB is the minimum energy. Thus, when {tex}\vec M{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are parallel to each other bar magnet is in stable equilibrium.
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment M = 0.9 J/T
    {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.063 J, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o 
    We know {tex}\tau=M \times B{/tex} 
    = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 
    0.063 = 0.9 {tex}\times{/tex} B {tex}\times{/tex} sin 30o 
    B = {tex}\frac{2 \times 0.063}{0.9}{/tex} = 0.14 T
  2. Stable equilibrium is position of minimum energy. Since {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} 
    U = - MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} 
    Where U is the energy stored or P.E. of the magnet inside magnetic field B.
    So, when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0, U = -MB is the minimum energy.
    Thus, when {tex}\vec M{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} are parallel to each other bar magnet is in stable equilibrium.

question_html:
A short bar magnet placed with its axis at 30° to a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T experiences a torque of 0.06 Nm.

  1. Calculate the magnetic moment of the magnet,
  2. Find out what orientation of the magnet corresponds to its stable equilibrium in the magnetic field.
question_text: A short bar magnet placed with its axis at 30° to a uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T experiences a torque of 0.06 Nm. Calculate the magnetic moment of the magnet, Find out what orientation of the magnet corresponds to its stable equilibrium in the magnetic field.
explanation: Here B = 0.2 T, {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o , {tex}\tau{/tex} = 0.06 Nm Magnetic moment, m = {tex}\frac{\tau}{B \sin \theta}=\frac{0.06}{0.2 \sin 30^{\circ}}=\frac{0.06}{0.2 \times 0.5}{/tex} = 0.6 Am 2 The P.E. of a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field is U = -mB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} In stable equilibrium, the P.E. is minimum. So, cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1 or {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o Hence the bar magnet will be in stable equilibrium when its magnetic moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} is parallel to the magnetic field {tex}\vec B{/tex} .

question_text: A magnetic dipole is placed in the position of stable equilibrium in a uniform electric field B. How much is the potential energy of the magnet? If it is rotated through 180°, then what will be the amount of work done?
explanation: In stable equilibrium, U = -mB cos 0° = -mB When the dipole is rotated through 180°, the work done is W = - mB (cos 180° - cos 0°) = - mB (- 1 - 1) = + 2mB

question_html: A short bar magnet placed in a horizontal plane has its axis aligned along the magnetic north-south direction. Null points are found on the axis of the magnet at 14 cm from the centre of the magnet. The earth’s magnetic field at the place is 0.36 G and the angle of dip is zero. What is the total magnetic field on the normal bisector of the magnet at the same distance as the null–point (i.e., 14 cm) from the centre of the magnet? (At null points, field due to a magnet is equal and opposite to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field.)
question_text: A short bar magnet placed in a horizontal plane has its axis aligned along the magnetic north-south direction. Null points are found on the axis of the magnet at 14 cm from the centre of the magnet. The earth’s magnetic field at the place is 0.36 G and the angle of dip is zero. What is the total magnetic field on the normal bisector of the magnet at the same distance as the null–point (i.e., 14 cm) from the centre of the magnet? (At null points, field due to a magnet is equal and opposite to the horizontal component of earth’s magnetic field.)
explanation: Earth's magnetic field at the given place, H = 0.36G The magnetic field at a distance d, on the axis of the magnetic is given as: {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{d^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i) where, {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space M = Magnetic moment The magnetic field at the same distance d, on the equatorial line of the magnet is given as: {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}}=\frac{H}{2}{/tex} [Using equation (i)] Total magnetic field on the normal bisector is given by, B = B 1 + B 2 {tex}=H+\frac{H}{2}{/tex} = 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54G Hence, the magnetic field is 0.54 G in the direction of earth's magnetic field.
solution: Earth's magnetic field at the given place, H = 0.36G The magnetic field at a distance d, on the axis of the magnetic is given as: {tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{d^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i) where, {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space M = Magnetic moment The magnetic field at the same distance d, on the equatorial line of the magnet is given as: {tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}}=\frac{H}{2}{/tex} [Using equation (i)] Total magnetic field on the normal bisector is given by, B = B 1 + B 2 {tex}=H+\frac{H}{2}{/tex} = 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54G Hence, the magnetic field is 0.54 G in the direction of earth's magnetic field.
solution_html:
Earth's magnetic field at the given place, H = 0.36G
The magnetic field at a distance d, on the axis of the magnetic is given as:
{tex}B_{1}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{d^{3}}=H{/tex} .... (i)
where,
{tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Permeability of free space
M = Magnetic moment
The magnetic field at the same distance d, on the equatorial line of the magnet is given as:
{tex}B_{2}=\frac{\mu_{0} M}{4 \pi d^{3}}=\frac{H}{2}{/tex} [Using equation (i)]
Total magnetic field on the normal bisector is given by, B = B1 + B2
{tex}=H+\frac{H}{2}{/tex}
= 0.36 + 0.18 = 0.54G
Hence, the magnetic field is 0.54 G in the direction of earth's magnetic field.

solution: The work done in turning a dipole from orientation {tex}\theta_1{/tex} to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} in field B is given by W = -MB (cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} ) Here, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0 o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = - MB (cos 180 o - cos 0 o ) = -MB (-1 - 1) = 2 MB Here, {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0 o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = - MB (cos 900 o - cos 0 o ) = MB
solution_html:
The work done in turning a dipole from orientation {tex}\theta_1{/tex} to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} in field B is given by
W = -MB (cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex})

question_html: What do you mean by diamagnetism, paramagnetism and ferromagnetism?
question_text: What do you mean by diamagnetism, paramagnetism and ferromagnetism?
explanation: Diamagnetic Diamagnetic substances have a tendency to move from the stronger part to the weaker part of the external magnetic field. We can also say that the diamagnetic substances get repelled by a magnet. Paramagnetism Paramagnetic substances are those substances that get weakly magnetized in the presence of an external magnetic field. In the presence of an external magnetic field, these substances tend to move from a region of a weak to a strong magnetic field. In other terms, we can say that these substances tend to get weakly attracted to a permanent magnet. In a paramagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, which when placed in a magnetic field, interact with one another, and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction, which results in its magnetization. Ferromagnetism Ferromagnetic substances are those substances that when it’s placed in an external magnetic field, get strongly magnetized. Also, they tend to move from a region of weak to the region of a strong magnetic field and get strongly attracted to a magnet. In a ferromagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, similar to a paramagnetic material. When placed in a magnetic field, the atoms interact with one another and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction.

Ferromagnetism Ferromagnetic substances are those substances that when it’s placed in an external magnetic field, get strongly magnetized. Also, they tend to move from a region of weak to the region of a strong magnetic field and get strongly attracted to a magnet. In a ferromagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, similar to a paramagnetic material. When placed in a magnetic field, the atoms interact with one another and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction.
solution_html:
Diamagnetic

Diamagnetic substances have a tendency to move from the stronger part to the weaker part of the external magnetic field. We can also say that the diamagnetic substances get repelled by a magnet.

Paramagnetism

Paramagnetic substances are those substances that get weakly magnetized in the presence of an external magnetic field. In the presence of an external magnetic field, these substances tend to move from a region of a weak to a strong magnetic field. In other terms, we can say that these substances tend to get weakly attracted to a permanent magnet. In a paramagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, which when placed in a magnetic field, interact with one another, and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction, which results in its magnetization.

Ferromagnetism

Ferromagnetic substances are those substances that when it’s placed in an external magnetic field, get strongly magnetized. Also, they tend to move from a region of weak to the region of a strong magnetic field and get strongly attracted to a magnet. In a ferromagnetic material, the individual atoms possess a dipole moment, similar to a paramagnetic material. When placed in a magnetic field, the atoms interact with one another and get spontaneously aligned in a common direction.

question_html: What are magnetic lines of force? Give their important properties.
question_text: What are magnetic lines of force? Give their important properties.
explanation: A magnetic line of force may be defined as the curve the tangent to which at any point gives the direction of the magnetic field at that point. It may also be defined as the path along which a unit north pole would tend to move if free to do so. Properties of lines of force: Magnetic lines of force are closed curves which start in air from the N-pole and end at the S-pole and then return to the N-pole through the interior of the magnet. The lines of force never cross each other. If they do so, that would mean there are two directions of the magnetic field at the point of intersection, which is impossible. They start from and end on the surface of the magnet normally. The lines of force have a tendency to contract lengthwise and expand sidewise. This explains attraction between unlike poles and repulsion between like poles. The relative closeness of the lines of force gives a measure of the strength of the magnetic field which is maximum at the poles.

question_text: Two identical bar magnets P and Q are placed in two identical uniform magnetic fields as shown in figure. Justify that both the magnets are in equilibrium. Which one of these is in stable equilibrium? Give reasons for your answer.
explanation: Let q m be the pole strength of each magnet. In case of magnet P, Force on N-pole = q m B (towards right) Force on S-pole = q m B (towards left) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on P = 0 The dipole moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} of P points in the opposite direction of {tex}\vec B{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin 180 o = 0 P.E. of P = - mB cos 180° = + mB In case of magnet Q , Force on N-pole = qm B (towards right) Force on S-pole = q m B (towards left) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on Q = 0 The dipole moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} of Q points in the direction {tex}\vec B{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin 0° = 0 P.E. of Q = - mB cos 0° = - mB As the net force and net torque on both of the magnets are zero, so both P and Q are in equilibrium. But Q is in stable equilibrium because its P.E. is minimum. If the magnet P (in unstable equilibrium) is disturbed, a torque acts on it to align it in the opposite direction, whereas the torque on magnet Q brings it back to the original alignment.

Force on N-pole = qm B (towards right)
Force on S-pole = qm B (towards left)

P.E. of P = - mB cos 180° = + mB
In case of magnet Q,
Force on N-pole = qm B (towards right)
Force on S-pole = qm B (towards left)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Net force on Q = 0
The dipole moment {tex}\vec m{/tex} of Q points in the direction {tex}\vec B{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = mB sin 0° = 0
P.E. of Q = - mB cos 0° = - mB

question_html: What are paramagnetic substances? Explain the origin of paramagnetism.
question_text: What are paramagnetic substances? Explain the origin of paramagnetism.
explanation: Paramagnetic substances: Paramagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Origin of paramagnetism: According to Langevin, the atoms or molecules of a paramagnetic material possess a permanent magnetic moment either due to the presence of some unpaired electron or due to the non-cancellation of the spins of two electrons because of some special reason. In the absence of an external magnetic field, the atomic dipoles are randomly oriented due to their ceaseless random motion, as shown in figure (a). There is no net magnetisation. When a strong enough field {tex}\vec B_0{/tex} , is applied and the temperature is low enough, the field {tex}\vec B_0{/tex} tends to align the atomic dipoles in its own direction, producing a weak magnetic moment in the direction of {tex}\vec B_0{/tex} . The material tends to move from a weak field region to a strong field region. This is paramagnetism. At very high magnetic fields or at very low temperatures, the magnetisation approaches its maximum value when all the atomic dipole moments get aligned. This is called the saturation magnetisation value Ms.

question_html: Classify materials on the basis of their behaviour in a magnetic field. Under which category does iron come? How does the magnetic property of iron change with the increase of temperature?
question_text: Classify materials on the basis of their behaviour in a magnetic field. Under which category does iron come? How does the magnetic property of iron change with the increase of temperature?
explanation: Classification of magnetic materials: On the basis of their behaviour in external magnetic fields, Faraday classified the various substances into three categories: Diamagnetic substances: Diamagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the opposite direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly repelled by magnets and tend to move from stronger to weaker parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Bismuth, copper, lead, zinc, tin, gold, silicon, nitrogen (at STP), water, sodium chloride, etc. Paramagnetic substances: Paramagnetic substances are those which develop feeble magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. Such substances are feebly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Manganese, aluminium, chromium, platinum, sodium, copper chloride, oxygen (it STP), etc. Ferromagnetic substances: Ferromagnetic substances are those which develop strong magnetisation in the direction of the magnetising field. They are strongly attracted by magnets and tend to move from weaker to stronger parts of a magnetic field. Examples. Iron, cobalt, nickel, gadolinium and alloys like alnico. Iron is a ferromagnetic substance. As temperature increases, its magnetisation decreases due to the randomisation of its domains.

question_html: A small compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is free to oscillate in a magnetic field B. It is slightly disturbed from its equilibrium position and then released. Show that it executes simple harmonic motion. Write the expression for its time period.
question_text: A small compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is free to oscillate in a magnetic field B. It is slightly disturbed from its equilibrium position and then released. Show that it executes simple harmonic motion. Write the expression for its time period.
explanation: When compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is slightly disturbed by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the mean position of equilibrium. Then, restoring torque begins to act on the needle which try to bring the needle back to its mean position which is given by {tex}\vec {\tau} = - \vec M× \vec B{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \tau = - M B \sin \theta{/tex} (taking only magnitude), B being magnetic field intensity. Since, {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, so, {tex}\sin \theta = \theta{/tex} {tex}\tau = - M B \theta{/tex} ...... (i) But {tex}\tau = I \alpha{/tex} ...... (ii) where, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = angular acceleration, I = moment of inertia of the needle and M = magnetic moment of dipole On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), {tex}\Rightarrow I \alpha = - M B \theta \Rightarrow \alpha = - ( M B / I ) \theta {/tex} .....(iii) Thus, angular acceleration {tex}\alpha {/tex} is directly proportional to angular displacement {tex}\theta{/tex} . Therefore, the needle will execute SHM. Comparing equation (iii) with the general form of SHM, {tex}\alpha = - {\omega ^2}\theta{/tex} , we get {tex}\omega = \sqrt {\frac {MB}{I}}{/tex} Hence, the time period, {tex}T = \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } = \frac { 2 \pi } { \sqrt { M B / I } } \text { or } \quad T = 2 \pi \sqrt { \frac { I } { M B } }{/tex} This is the required expression.

Comparing equation (iii) with the general form of SHM, {tex}\alpha = - {\omega ^2}\theta{/tex} , we get {tex}\omega = \sqrt {\frac {MB}{I}}{/tex} Hence, the time period, {tex}T = \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } = \frac { 2 \pi } { \sqrt { M B / I } } \text { or } \quad T = 2 \pi \sqrt { \frac { I } { M B } }{/tex} This is the required expression.
solution_html:
When compass needle of magnetic moment M and moment of inertia I is slightly disturbed by an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the mean position of equilibrium. Then, restoring torque begins to act on the needle which try to bring the needle back to its mean position which is given by
{tex}\vec {\tau} = - \vec M× \vec B{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \tau = - M B \sin \theta{/tex} (taking only magnitude), B being magnetic field intensity.
Since, {tex}\theta {/tex} is small, so, {tex}\sin \theta = \theta{/tex}
{tex}\tau = - M B \theta{/tex} ...... (i)
But {tex}\tau = I \alpha{/tex} ...... (ii)
where, {tex}\alpha {/tex} = angular acceleration, I = moment of inertia of the needle and M = magnetic moment of dipole
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii),
{tex}\Rightarrow I \alpha = - M B \theta \Rightarrow \alpha = - ( M B / I ) \theta {/tex} .....(iii)
Thus, angular acceleration {tex}\alpha {/tex} is directly proportional to angular displacement {tex}\theta{/tex}. Therefore, the needle will execute SHM.
Comparing equation (iii) with the general form of SHM, {tex}\alpha = - {\omega ^2}\theta{/tex}, we get
{tex}\omega = \sqrt {\frac {MB}{I}}{/tex}
Hence, the time period,
{tex}T = \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } = \frac { 2 \pi } { \sqrt { M B / I } } \text { or } \quad T = 2 \pi \sqrt { \frac { I } { M B } }{/tex}
This is the required expression.

question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 6 J/T is aligned at 60 o with a uniform external magnetic field of 0.44 T. Calculate the work done in turning the magnet to align its magnetic moment normal to the magnetic field opposite to the magnetic field the torque on the magnet in the final orientation in case (ii).

solution_html:
1. When a dipole is rotated from 1 position to other position 2 in a uniform magnetic field,
    Workdone, W = mB(cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex})
    (i) Here {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60o, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90o 
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Workdone, W = mB(cos 60o - cos 90o)
    = mB({tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} - 0) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mB
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 = 1.32 J

    (ii) Here {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 60o, {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180o

  2. Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = {tex}|\vec{m} \times \overline{B}|{/tex} = mB sin{tex}\theta{/tex} 
    For {tex}\theta{/tex} = 180o, we have
    Torque, {tex}\tau{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.44 sin 180o = 0

question_html:
1.  A small compass needle of magnetic moment m is free to turn about an axis perpendicular to the direction of uniform magnetic field B. The moment of inertia of the needle about the axis is I. The needle is slightly disturbed from its stable position and then released. Prove that it executes simple harmonic motion. Hence, deduce the expression for its time period.
  2. Prove that the potential energy of a magnet in a uniform magnetic field is {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}.
question_text: A small compass needle of magnetic moment m is free to turn about an axis perpendicular to the direction of uniform magnetic field B . The moment of inertia of the needle about the axis is I . The needle is slightly disturbed from its stable position and then released. Prove that it executes simple harmonic motion. Hence, deduce the expression for its time period. Prove that the potential energy of a magnet in a uniform magnetic field is {tex}U=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} .
explanation: Let magnetic needle is suspended in the uniform external magnetic field B and making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it and is slightly disturbed to oscillatein this field. As Magnetic moment {tex}\vec M{/tex} is directed along the axisof needle, torque {tex}\tau{/tex} on the needle is {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} We also know that {tex}\tau=I \alpha{/tex} Where I is moment of inertia of needle and {tex}\alpha{/tex} angular acceleration Angular acceleration, {tex}\alpha=\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\tau=I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \sin \theta{/tex} (-ve sign implies that this torque is acting as restoring torque). Now if {tex}\theta{/tex} is small,then sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} So, {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \theta{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\left(\frac{-M B}{I}\right) \theta{/tex} ...(2) This derived equation is similar to equation of simple harmonic motion, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\omega^2=\frac{M B}{I}{/tex} Hence, it is proved that it executes simple harmonic motion. We know, that {tex}\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} where, T is period of oscillation. {tex}\therefore \sqrt{\frac{M B}{I}}=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex} The magnetic potential energy {tex}=U=\int \tau d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int M B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} = -MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}
solution: Let magnetic needle is suspended in the uniform external magnetic field B and making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it and is slightly disturbed to oscillatein this field. As Magnetic moment {tex}\vec M{/tex} is directed along the axisof needle, torque {tex}\tau{/tex} on the needle is {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}{/tex} = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex} We also know that {tex}\tau=I \alpha{/tex} Where I is moment of inertia of needle and {tex}\alpha{/tex} angular acceleration Angular acceleration, {tex}\alpha=\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\tau=I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \sin \theta{/tex} (-ve sign implies that this torque is acting as restoring torque). Now if {tex}\theta{/tex} is small,then sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex} So, {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \theta{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\left(\frac{-M B}{I}\right) \theta{/tex} ...(2) This derived equation is similar to equation of simple harmonic motion, {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \theta{/tex} where, {tex}\omega^2=\frac{M B}{I}{/tex} Hence, it is proved that it executes simple harmonic motion. We know, that {tex}\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} where, T is period of oscillation. {tex}\therefore \sqrt{\frac{M B}{I}}=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex} {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex} The magnetic potential energy {tex}=U=\int \tau d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int M B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} = -MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Let magnetic needle is suspended in the uniform external magnetic field B and making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with it and is slightly disturbed to oscillatein this field.
    As Magnetic moment {tex}\vec M{/tex} is directed along the axisof needle, torque {tex}\tau{/tex} on the needle is
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{M} \times \vec{B}{/tex}
    = MB sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
    We also know that 
    {tex}\tau=I \alpha{/tex}
    Where I is moment of inertia of needle and {tex}\alpha{/tex} angular acceleration
    Angular acceleration,
    {tex}\alpha=\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\tau=I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \sin \theta{/tex}
    (-ve sign implies that this torque is acting as restoring torque).
    Now if {tex}\theta{/tex} is small,then sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\theta{/tex}
    So, {tex}I \frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-M B \theta{/tex}
    or,  {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=\left(\frac{-M B}{I}\right) \theta{/tex} ...(2)
    This derived equation is similar to equation of simple harmonic motion,
    {tex}\frac{d^2 \theta}{d t^2}=-\omega^2 \theta{/tex}
    where, {tex}\omega^2=\frac{M B}{I}{/tex}
    Hence, it is proved that it executes simple harmonic motion.
    We know, that {tex}\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex}
    where, T is period of oscillation. 
    {tex}\therefore \sqrt{\frac{M B}{I}}=\frac{2 \pi}{T}{/tex}
    {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}{/tex}
  2. The magnetic potential energy
    {tex}=U=\int \tau d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\int M B \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    = -MB cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    {tex}=-\vec{M} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html:
1. What happens if a bar magnet is cut into two pieces
    1. transverse to its length
    2. along its length?
  2. What happens if an iron bar magnet is melted? Does it retain its magnetism?
  3. A magnetised needle in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque but no net force, However, an iron nail near a bar magnet experiences a force of attraction in addition to torque, explain.
  4. Must every magnetic field configuration have a north pole and a south pole? What about the field due to a toroid?
  5. Can you think of a magnetic field configuration with three poles?
question_text: What happens if a bar magnet is cut into two pieces transverse to its length along its length? What happens if an iron bar magnet is melted? Does it retain its magnetism? A magnetised needle in a uniform magnetic field experiences a torque but no net force, However, an iron nail near a bar magnet experiences a force of attraction in addition to torque, explain. Must every magnetic field configuration have a north pole and a south pole? What about the field due to a toroid? Can you think of a magnetic field configuration with three poles?
explanation: In both cases, we get magnets each having north and south poles. When we cut transverse to the length, the pole strength of each new magnet is the same as that of an original magnet but the magnetic moment is halved because length is halved. When we cut along the length, the pole strength of each new magnet is half the pole strength of the original magnet; the magnetic moment is also halved, as length remains the same. On melting, the iron bar magnet loses its magnetism. This is because of its temperature exceeds Curie temperature(= 750°C) for iron. When a magnetised needle is put in a uniform magnetic field, forces on north and south poles the needle are equal and unlike. Therefore, the net force is zero. But these forces form a torque that aligns the magnetic needle in the direction of the field. An iron nail is unmagnetised. It experiences a force of attraction; gets magnetised, then experiences a torque and gets aligned along the field. No, it is not necessary that every magnetic field configuration must have a north pole and a south pole. The poles exist only when the source has some net magnetic moment. In the case of a toroid and infinite straight conductor, there are no poles, as a net magnetic moment in both the cases is zero. Magnetic poles always exist in pairs. However, one can imagine magnetic field configuration with three poles-when north poles of two magnets are glued together or south poles of two magnets are glued together to provide a three-pole field configuration.
solution: In both cases, we get magnets each having north and south poles. When we cut transverse to the length, the pole strength of each new magnet is the same as that of an original magnet but the magnetic moment is halved because length is halved. When we cut along the length, the pole strength of each new magnet is half the pole strength of the original magnet; the magnetic moment is also halved, as length remains the same. On melting, the iron bar magnet loses its magnetism. This is because of its temperature exceeds Curie temperature(= 750°C) for iron. When a magnetised needle is put in a uniform magnetic field, forces on north and south poles the needle are equal and unlike. Therefore, the net force is zero. But these forces form a torque that aligns the magnetic needle in the direction of the field. An iron nail is unmagnetised. It experiences a force of attraction; gets magnetised, then experiences a torque and gets aligned along the field. No, it is not necessary that every magnetic field configuration must have a north pole and a south pole. The poles exist only when the source has some net magnetic moment. In the case of a toroid and infinite straight conductor, there are no poles, as a net magnetic moment in both the cases is zero. Magnetic poles always exist in pairs. However, one can imagine magnetic field configuration with three poles-when north poles of two magnets are glued together or south poles of two magnets are glued together to provide a three-pole field configuration.
solution_html:
1. In both cases, we get magnets each having north and south poles. When we cut transverse to the length, the pole strength of each new magnet is the same as that of an original magnet but the magnetic moment is halved because length is halved. When we cut along the length, the pole strength of each new magnet is half the pole strength of the original magnet; the magnetic moment is also halved, as length remains the same.
  2. On melting, the iron bar magnet loses its magnetism. This is because of its temperature exceeds Curie temperature(= 750°C) for iron.
  3. When a magnetised needle is put in a uniform magnetic field, forces on north and south poles the needle are equal and unlike. Therefore, the net force is zero. But these forces form a torque that aligns the magnetic needle in the direction of the field. An iron nail is unmagnetised. It experiences a force of attraction; gets magnetised, then experiences a torque and gets aligned along the field.
  4. No, it is not necessary that every magnetic field configuration must have a north pole and a south pole. The poles exist only when the source has some net magnetic moment. In the case of a toroid and infinite straight conductor, there are no poles, as a net magnetic moment in both the cases is zero.
  5. Magnetic poles always exist in pairs. However, one can imagine magnetic field configuration with three poles-when north poles of two magnets are glued together or south poles of two magnets are glued together to provide a three-pole field configuration.

question_html:
A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J T-1 lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T.

  1. What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment (i) normal to the field direction, (ii) opposite to the field direction?
  2. What is the torque on the magnet in cases (i) and (ii)?
question_text: A bar magnet of magnetic moment 1.5 J T -1 lies aligned with the direction of a uniform magnetic field of 0.22 T. What is the amount of work required by an external torque to turn the magnet so as to align its magnetic moment (i) normal to the field direction, (ii) opposite to the field direction? What is the torque on the magnet in cases (i) and (ii)?
explanation: Magnetic moment, M = {tex}1.5 J T^{-1}{/tex} Magnetic field strength, B = 0.22 T The work required to align the magnetic moment normal to the direction of magnetic field is given as: {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}=-0.33(0-1){/tex} {tex}=0.33 J{/tex} The work required to make the magnetic moment opposite to the direction of magnetic field ( {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180°) is given as: {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 180^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}=-0.33(-1-1){/tex} {tex}=0.66 J{/tex} For case (i): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=1.5 \times 0.22 \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=0.33 J{/tex} For case (ii): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=M B \sin 180^{\circ}=0 J{/tex}
solution: Magnetic moment, M = {tex}1.5 J T^{-1}{/tex} Magnetic field strength, B = 0.22 T The work required to align the magnetic moment normal to the direction of magnetic field is given as: {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}=-0.33(0-1){/tex} {tex}=0.33 J{/tex} The work required to make the magnetic moment opposite to the direction of magnetic field ( {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180°) is given as: {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 180^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex} {tex}=-0.33(-1-1){/tex} {tex}=0.66 J{/tex} For case (i): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=1.5 \times 0.22 \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}=0.33 J{/tex} For case (ii): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=180^{\circ}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}=M B \sin 180^{\circ}=0 J{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Magnetic moment, M = {tex}1.5 J T^{-1}{/tex} 
    Magnetic field strength, B = 0.22 T
    1. The work required to align the magnetic moment normal to the direction of magnetic field is given as:
      {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 90^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-0.33(0-1){/tex}
      {tex}=0.33 J{/tex}

    2. The work required to make the magnetic moment opposite to the direction of magnetic field ({tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0° and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 180°) is given as:
      {tex}W=-M B\left(\cos \theta_{2}-\cos \theta_{1}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-1.5 \times 0.22\left(\cos 180^{\circ}-\cos 0^{\circ}\right){/tex}
      {tex}=-0.33(-1-1){/tex}
      {tex}=0.66 J{/tex}

  2. For case (i): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=90^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=1.5 \times 0.22 \sin 90^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}=0.33 J{/tex}
    For case (ii): {tex}\theta=\theta_{2}=180^{\circ}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Torque, {tex}\tau=M B \sin \theta{/tex}{tex}=M B \sin 180^{\circ}=0 J{/tex}

subject: Physics
question_html: Derive an expression for magnetic field intensity due to a magnetic dipole at a point lies on its equatorial line.
question_text: Derive an expression for magnetic field intensity due to a magnetic dipole at a point lies on its equatorial line.
explanation: Consider a point P on equatorial position (or broad side on position) of short bar magnet of length 2l, having north pole (N) and south pole (S) of strength +q m and -q m respectively. The distance of point P from the mid point (O) of magnet is r. Let B 1 and B 2 be the magnetic field intensities due to north and south poles respectively. {tex}N P=S P=\sqrt{r^2+l^2}{/tex} . {tex}\vec{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{q_m}{r^2+l^2}{/tex} along N to P {tex}\vec{B}_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{q_m}{r^2+l^2}{/tex} along P to S Clearly, magnitudes of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} are equal i.e., {tex}\left|\vec{B}_1\right|=\vec{B}_2 \mid {/tex} or B 1 = B 2 To find the resultant of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} , we resolve them along and perpendicular to magnetic axis SN. Components of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} along and perpendicular to magnetic axis are B 1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and B 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} respectively. Components of {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} along and perpendicular to magnetic axis are B 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and B 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} respectively. Clearly, components of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} perpendicular to axis SN. B 1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} and B 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction and hence, cancel each other; while the components of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} along the axis are in the same direction ani hence, add up to give to resultant magnetic field parallel to the direction {tex}\overrightarrow{N S}{/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant magnetic field intensity at P. solution_html:
Consider a point P on equatorial position (or broad side on position) of short bar magnet of length 2l, having north pole (N) and south pole (S) of strength +qm and -qm respectively. The distance of point P from the mid point (O) of magnet is r. Let B1 and B2 be the magnetic field intensities due to north and south poles respectively. {tex}N P=S P=\sqrt{r^2+l^2}{/tex}.
{tex}\vec{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{q_m}{r^2+l^2}{/tex} along N to P
{tex}\vec{B}_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{q_m}{r^2+l^2}{/tex} along P to S
Clearly, magnitudes of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} are equal
i.e., {tex}\left|\vec{B}_1\right|=\vec{B}_2 \mid {/tex} or B1 = B2

To find the resultant of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex}, we resolve them along and perpendicular to magnetic axis SN. Components of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} along and perpendicular to magnetic axis are B1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and B2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} respectively.
Components of {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} along and perpendicular to magnetic axis are B2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and B2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} respectively. Clearly, components of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} perpendicular to axis SN. B1 sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
and B2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction and hence, cancel each other; while the components of {tex}\vec{B}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}_2{/tex} along the axis are in the same direction ani hence, add up to give to resultant magnetic field parallel to the direction {tex}\overrightarrow{N S}{/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Resultant magnetic field intensity at P.
B = B1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + B2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
But {tex}\quad B_1=B_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{q_m}{r^2+l^2}{/tex} and {tex}\cos \theta=\frac{O N}{P N}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{r^2+l^2}}=\frac{l}{\left(r^2+l^2\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} B = 2B1 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{q_m}{\left(r^2+l^2\right)} \times \frac{l}{\left(r^2+l^2\right)^\frac{1 }{ 2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 q_m l}{\left(r^2+l^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex}
But qm{tex}\cdot {/tex}2l=m, magnetic moment of magnet
{tex}\therefore B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{m}{\left(r^2+l^2\right)^\frac{3 }{ 2}}{/tex} ...(i)
If the magnet is very short and point P is far away, we have l< < r; so l2 may be neglected as compared to r2 and so equation (3) takes the form
{tex}B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{m}{r^3}{/tex} ...(ii)
This is expression for magnetic field intensity at equatorial position of the magnet.
